quiz_questions.question_text: Which intermolecular force is present in ALL molecules, regardless of polarity?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces
Hydrogen bonding
London dispersion forces
Dipole-dipole forces

quiz_questions.explanation: London dispersion forces (LDF) arise from temporary fluctuations in electron distribution and are present in all molecules and atoms. They are the only IMF in nonpolar substances.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hydrogen bonding occurs when hydrogen is bonded to which of the following elements?

options.text: C, Si, or Ge
Any halogen
F, O, or N
S, P, or Cl

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds form when H is bonded to highly electronegative atoms with lone pairs: fluorine, oxygen, or nitrogen (FON). These create a strong partial positive charge on H that attracts lone pairs on neighboring molecules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which substance would have the highest boiling point?

options.text: GeH₄ (77 g/mol)
SiH₄ (32 g/mol)
H₂O (18 g/mol)
CH₄ (16 g/mol)

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite having the lowest molar mass, H₂O has the highest boiling point due to strong hydrogen bonding between molecules. The other substances only have London dispersion forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of IMF exists between HCl molecules?

options.text: Dipole-dipole forces and London dispersion forces
Ion-dipole forces
London dispersion forces only
Hydrogen bonding

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl is a polar molecule (dipole-dipole forces) and, like all molecules, also has London dispersion forces. It does not have hydrogen bonding because Cl is not sufficiently electronegative (not F, O, or N).

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does hexane (C₆H₁₄) have a higher boiling point than methane (CH₄)?

options.text: Hexane has ionic character
Hexane is polar while methane is nonpolar
Hexane has stronger London dispersion forces due to more electrons and greater surface area
Hexane has hydrogen bonds while methane does not

quiz_questions.explanation: Both hexane and methane are nonpolar and only have LDF. However, hexane has more electrons (50 vs 10) and greater surface area, creating stronger temporary dipoles and therefore stronger London dispersion forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following liquids has the highest surface tension?

options.text: Acetone
Hexane
Ethanol
Water

quiz_questions.explanation: Water has the highest surface tension among common liquids due to its extensive hydrogen bonding network. Each water molecule can form up to 4 hydrogen bonds, creating strong cohesive forces at the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ion-dipole forces are most important in which situation?

options.text: Between two identical polar molecules
When an ionic compound dissolves in a polar solvent like water
Between two nonpolar molecules
In metallic bonding

quiz_questions.explanation: Ion-dipole forces occur between ions and polar molecules. They are the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water, as water molecules surround and stabilize the separated ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about the boiling points of noble gases is correct?

options.text: Boiling points increase going down the group due to increasing London dispersion forces
All noble gases have the same boiling point
Boiling points decrease going down the group
Noble gases cannot be liquefied

quiz_questions.explanation: Noble gases only experience London dispersion forces. Going down the group, atomic size and electron count increase, creating stronger temporary dipoles and increasing boiling points (He: -269°C to Rn: -62°C).

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is the boiling point of ethanol (78°C) much higher than that of dimethyl ether (−24°C), even though they have the same molecular formula C₂H₆O?

options.text: Ethanol can form hydrogen bonds through its O-H group, while dimethyl ether cannot
Ethanol has a higher molecular weight
Ethanol has stronger London dispersion forces
Dimethyl ether is nonpolar

quiz_questions.explanation: Ethanol has an -OH group that can form hydrogen bonds (H bonded to O). Dimethyl ether (CH₃-O-CH₃) has no O-H or N-H bonds, so it cannot form hydrogen bonds, resulting in a much lower boiling point.

quiz_questions.question_text: Viscosity of a liquid generally increases with:

options.text: Increasing temperature and weaker IMFs
Increasing temperature and stronger IMFs
Decreasing pressure only
Decreasing temperature and stronger IMFs

quiz_questions.explanation: Viscosity increases with stronger intermolecular forces (more resistance to flow) and decreases with higher temperature (molecules move faster and overcome IMFs more easily).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'London dispersion forces'?

options.text: The force holding atoms together within a molecule
Forces between permanently polar molecules
Forces between ions and polar solvents
Temporary attractive forces caused by instantaneous dipoles from fluctuations in electron distribution, present in all molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: London dispersion forces (also called van der Waals forces) arise from temporary, instantaneous dipoles created by random electron movement. They increase with molecular size, electron count, and surface area.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dipole-dipole force' mean?

options.text: The intramolecular force within a polar bond
The force between two nonpolar molecules
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule
The attractive force between the positive end of one polar molecule and the negative end of another polar molecule

quiz_questions.explanation: Dipole-dipole forces are attractions between the partial positive end (δ+) of one polar molecule and the partial negative end (δ-) of another. They are stronger than LDF for molecules of similar size.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'hydrogen bond'?

options.text: Any bond involving hydrogen
A covalent bond between two hydrogen atoms
A strong dipole-dipole attraction between a hydrogen atom bonded to F, O, or N and a lone pair on a nearby F, O, or N atom
An ionic bond between hydrogen and a halogen

quiz_questions.explanation: A hydrogen bond is an especially strong dipole-dipole force occurring when H is bonded to F, O, or N (highly electronegative atoms with lone pairs). It is about 5-10% the strength of a typical covalent bond.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'polarizability' mean?

options.text: The electronegativity difference between bonded atoms
The ease with which the electron cloud of a molecule can be distorted to form temporary dipoles
The permanent dipole moment of a molecule
The ability of a substance to dissolve in water

quiz_questions.explanation: Polarizability measures how easily an electron cloud can be distorted. Larger atoms/molecules with more electrons are more polarizable, leading to stronger London dispersion forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'surface tension'?

options.text: The tendency of a liquid to climb up a narrow tube
The force required to break a chemical bond
The energy required to increase the surface area of a liquid, caused by the net inward pull of intermolecular forces on surface molecules
The pressure exerted by a gas on its container

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface tension results from the imbalance of intermolecular forces on surface molecules (pulled inward but not outward). Liquids with stronger IMFs (like water with hydrogen bonds) have higher surface tension.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'capillary action' refer to?

options.text: The spreading of a drop of oil on water
The boiling of a liquid at reduced pressure
The resistance of a liquid to flow
The spontaneous rise or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube due to the interplay of adhesive and cohesive forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Capillary action occurs when adhesive forces (liquid-container attraction) compete with cohesive forces (liquid-liquid attraction). In water, adhesion to glass exceeds cohesion, causing the meniscus to rise.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'viscosity'?

options.text: A measure of a liquid's resistance to flow, determined by the strength of its intermolecular forces
The tendency of a liquid to form droplets
The density of a liquid compared to water
A measure of how easily a liquid evaporates

quiz_questions.explanation: Viscosity is the resistance of a fluid to flow. Liquids with strong IMFs and large, entangled molecules (like honey or motor oil) have high viscosity. Viscosity decreases with increasing temperature.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ion-dipole force' mean?

options.text: The force between two polar molecules
The attractive force between an ion and the partial charge of a polar molecule
The repulsion between like charges in solution
The force between two ions in a crystal

quiz_questions.explanation: Ion-dipole forces are the attraction between an ion (full charge) and a polar molecule (partial charge). They are the primary force responsible for dissolving ionic compounds in polar solvents like water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vapor pressure' mean?

options.text: The atmospheric pressure at sea level
The pressure exerted by the vapor of a liquid in equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given temperature
The pressure exerted by a gas above its boiling point
The maximum pressure a container can withstand

quiz_questions.explanation: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor in dynamic equilibrium with its liquid. Substances with weak IMFs have high vapor pressures (volatile) because molecules escape the liquid easily.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cohesion' versus 'adhesion' mean?

options.text: Cohesion is attraction between unlike substances; adhesion is attraction between like substances
Cohesion applies only to solids; adhesion applies only to liquids
Both terms mean the same thing
Cohesion is the attraction between molecules of the same substance; adhesion is the attraction between molecules of different substances

quiz_questions.explanation: Cohesion is the intermolecular attraction between identical molecules (water-water). Adhesion is the attraction between different molecules (water-glass). Their relative strength determines meniscus shape and capillary action.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gecko can walk upside down on a glass ceiling. Scientists discovered that billions of tiny hair-like structures (setae) on gecko feet maximize surface contact area. Which intermolecular force primarily allows geckos to adhere to surfaces?

options.text: Hydrogen bonding between gecko feet and the surface
Ionic bonds between the gecko and the glass surface
Suction created by the setae acting like tiny cups
London dispersion forces, which become significant when billions of setae maximize the contact surface area

quiz_questions.explanation: Gecko adhesion is primarily due to London dispersion forces. While individually weak, the billions of setae create an enormous contact surface area, and the cumulative LDF is strong enough to support the gecko's weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemical engineer is designing a new waterproof coating for outdoor gear. She knows that materials with weak IMFs between the coating and water will repel water (hydrophobic). Which type of polymer surface would best repel water?

options.text: A fluorinated polymer like PTFE (Teflon) with only weak London dispersion forces
A polymer with many -OH groups that can hydrogen bond with water
A polymer with ionic groups that attract water strongly
A polymer containing many N-H bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: PTFE (Teflon) is nonpolar with only weak LDF between its surface and water. Since water molecules prefer to hydrogen bond with each other rather than interact weakly with the PTFE surface, water beads up and rolls off.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nurse administers rubbing alcohol (isopropanol) to a patient's skin before an injection. The patient feels a cooling sensation. What explains the cooling effect of evaporating alcohol?

options.text: The alcohol creates a barrier that blocks body heat
The highest-energy alcohol molecules escape first during evaporation, removing heat from the skin and lowering its temperature
The alcohol reacts endothermically with the skin
The alcohol is stored at a cold temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation is endothermic. The most energetic molecules escape the liquid, absorbing heat from the skin. Alcohol evaporates faster than water (weaker H-bonding), producing a noticeable cooling effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food scientist observes that cooking oil and vinegar (aqueous acetic acid) separate into two layers in a salad dressing. When she adds an emulsifier (like egg yolk lecithin), they stay mixed. How does the emulsifier work at the molecular level?

options.text: The emulsifier has both a polar (hydrophilic) end that interacts with vinegar and a nonpolar (hydrophobic) end that interacts with oil, bridging the two phases
The emulsifier breaks the covalent bonds in oil molecules
The emulsifier increases the temperature enough to dissolve oil in water
The emulsifier reacts chemically with both oil and vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: Emulsifiers are amphiphilic: one end forms hydrogen bonds/dipole interactions with the polar vinegar, while the other end interacts via LDF with the nonpolar oil. This stabilizes small oil droplets in the aqueous phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: An environmental scientist studies why mercury (Hg) forms nearly perfect spherical droplets on a lab bench while water spreads more. Both are liquids at room temperature. What IMF difference explains mercury's behavior?

options.text: Mercury has very strong metallic cohesive forces between its atoms, and very weak adhesion to the bench surface, causing it to minimize surface area as spheres
The bench surface repels mercury through ionic interactions
Mercury has hydrogen bonds while water does not
Mercury is denser than water, so gravity pulls it into spheres

quiz_questions.explanation: Mercury atoms have strong metallic bonding (cohesion) but very weak adhesion to most surfaces. Since cohesion dominates over adhesion, mercury minimizes its surface area by forming spherical droplets.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mountaineer boils water at a high-altitude camp and notices it boils at only 85°C instead of 100°C. The food takes longer to cook. What intermolecular force concept explains why water boils at a lower temperature at high altitude?

options.text: The cold air temperature reduces the IMF strength
Water molecules have weaker hydrogen bonds at high altitude
UV radiation at high altitude breaks hydrogen bonds
The lower atmospheric pressure at high altitude means less external pressure holding water molecules in the liquid phase, so they escape as vapor at a lower temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At high altitude, atmospheric pressure is lower, so water's vapor pressure reaches it at a lower temperature. The IMFs themselves don't change.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company develops a transdermal drug patch that delivers medication through the skin. The drug must be able to pass through the nonpolar lipid bilayer of skin cells. Which molecular property would make a drug molecule more effective for transdermal delivery?

options.text: Very high molecular weight to slow absorption
Being ionic to maximize solubility
High polarity and ability to form many hydrogen bonds
Moderate lipophilicity (nonpolar character) to pass through the lipid bilayer, combined with some polar character to dissolve in blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Effective transdermal drugs need a balance: enough nonpolar character (LDF interactions) to cross the lipid bilayer of skin, but enough polar character to dissolve in aqueous blood once absorbed. This is the concept of lipophilicity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A materials engineer is comparing two substances for use as a lubricant: a long-chain hydrocarbon (C₃₀H₆₂) and a short-chain hydrocarbon (C₈H₁₈). Both are nonpolar. Why would the long-chain hydrocarbon be a better lubricant?

options.text: Longer chains have polar regions that reduce friction
Shorter chains are too dense to work as lubricants
The longer chain has stronger LDF and higher viscosity due to more electrons and greater surface area, allowing it to maintain a protective film under pressure
Long-chain hydrocarbons have hydrogen bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Longer hydrocarbon chains have more electrons and greater surface area for LDF, resulting in higher viscosity. This thicker liquid maintains a continuous film between moving parts, providing better lubrication.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemistry student places a paper clip carefully on the surface of water and observes it floats despite being denser than water. She then adds a drop of soap and the paper clip sinks immediately. Why does soap cause the paper clip to sink?

options.text: Soap reacts with the metal of the paper clip
Soap makes the paper clip heavier by coating it
Soap molecules disrupt the hydrogen bonding network at the water surface, reducing surface tension so it can no longer support the paper clip
Soap increases the density of water

quiz_questions.explanation: The paper clip floats due to water's high surface tension (from hydrogen bonding). Soap is a surfactant that inserts between water molecules, breaking hydrogen bonds at the surface and reducing surface tension.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ice cream manufacturer knows that adding salt to ice creates a brine that can reach -21°C, cold enough to freeze the cream mixture. The freezing point depression depends on ion-dipole interactions. Why does NaCl dissolve in ice/water but not in pure ice?

options.text: The crystal structure of ice is too strong for NaCl to penetrate
Ice is too cold for NaCl to dissolve
NaCl reacts chemically with liquid water but not ice

In liquid water, mobile molecules can reorient to form ion-dipole interactions with Na⁺ and Cl⁻, stabilizing the dissolved ions; in rigid ice, water molecules are locked in place and cannot form these interactions

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissolving NaCl requires water molecules to surround ions through ion-dipole forces (hydration). In liquid water, molecules can freely reorient. In solid ice, the rigid hydrogen-bonding network prevents the molecular rearrangement needed for hydration.
